one_line_takeaway: DrillAgent shows that closing the loop between LLM semantic reasoning and concrete execution-state feedback (coverage-guided path exploration plus sanitizer-validated crash triggering, informed by structured root-cause analysis) roughly doubles validated PoV generation rate over a general-purpose LLM agent baseline (OpenHands) on real-world C/C++ CVEs under the same budget and backbone model, while cutting cost-per-validated-PoV by about half.
key_finding: On SEC-bench-Full ($1.5/task budget), DrillAgent generates 55 Validated PoVs (28.9% resolved rate) plus 12 Variant PoVs (67 total crashes, 35.3% crash rate) out of 190 tasks; this exceeds the previously reported SEC-bench leaderboard best of 36 solved tasks (18% resolved rate), a 52.8% relative increase, with 29 of the 55 solved tasks uniquely solved (not solved by any prior method). On the controlled SEC-bench-60 head-to-head, DrillAgent produces more than twice as many validated PoVs as OpenHands under the same $1.5/task budget and same backbone model (15 vs. 6); OpenHands only surpasses DrillAgent (18 vs. 15) when given an unlimited budget, at far higher cost. Cost efficiency (Table 2): DrillAgent averages $1.79/task (SEC-bench-Full) and $1.93/task (SEC-bench-60), translating to $7.72 per validated PoV on SEC-bench-60 -- versus $15.30/validated-PoV for budgeted OpenHands and $20.13 for unlimited-budget OpenHands. Average execution time is 11-12 min/task for DrillAgent versus 3.2 min (budgeted) / 7.7 min (unlimited) for OpenHands. Ablations (Figure 7, SEC-bench-Ablation-30, base = 30 validated PoVs) show removing root-cause analysis drops validated PoVs to 15, removing execution-state feedback drops to 17, and removing dedicated crash-triggering guidance (CTAgent) drops to 20 -- indicating RCA and execution feedback are the most load-bearing components, while crash-triggering guidance matters less for reachability but still aids the final triggering step. The similarity analysis (Table 4) found low average overlap (score 0.0296, max 0.1320 across 24 solved CVEs) between generated and ground-truth PoVs, offered as evidence against simple memorization/leakage of known public PoVs.
core_contribution: The paper presents DrillAgent, an agentic framework (four role-specialized LLM sub-agents: VAAgent, ITAgent, PEAgent, CTAgent) that automates end-to-end Proof-of-Vulnerability (PoV) generation for real-world C/C++ CVEs. It reformulates PoV generation as an iterative hypothesis-verification-refinement loop, closing the gap between LLM semantic reasoning and concrete execution state via three mechanisms: (1) a Vulnerability Analysis Agent that extracts crash backtraces, infers the harness command, and performs multi-angle root-cause analysis (RCA) to guide context retrieval; (2) an automated dual code-instrumentation pipeline (coverage-instrumented binary P_cov and sanitizer-instrumented binary P_san) with an instrumentation-effectiveness check; and (3) a two-phase generate-execute-refine loop split into Path Exploration (PEAgent, using fine-grained llvm-cov feedback to reach the vulnerable function) and Crash Triggering (CTAgent, using a vulnerability-type-specific prompt sampler to satisfy the final triggering predicate). The system is implemented from scratch (~7,700 LOC Python) and evaluated on SEC-bench, with cost-control techniques (model tiering between Claude Sonnet and Claude Haiku for different sub-tasks, tool-output length limits, budget monitoring).
threat_model: The paper does not target a network/adversary threat model in the traditional sense; instead it targets an automated-reproduction task: given a known reported vulnerability (location V_location and expected effect V_effect, e.g., a CVE with an optional sanitizer report) in a C/C++ open-source codebase, synthesize a concrete input I such that executing the program under a test harness both reaches V_location and triggers V_effect as confirmed by a sanitizer-based oracle. The scope is explicitly restricted to memory-safety vulnerabilities in C/C++ (the authors note the design could extend to logic vulnerabilities such as path traversal or command injection but this is left as future work, following FaultLine's approach). The paper frames the reproducer/agent itself as the 'defender-side' tool used for vulnerability validation, false-positive reduction in static analysis, and patch verification -- not as an offensive exploitation framework.
limitations: Authors' own Threats-to-Validity section: (1) Internal validity -- implementation bugs across the multi-agent pipeline could at worst cause generation failures (not false positives) since final validation always requires a sanitizer-confirmed crash on P_san, and most generated PoVs/logs were manually inspected; LLM temperature randomness (0.1 for most steps, 0.7 for generation steps) is only partially mitigated by amortizing over N iterations. (2) External validity -- risk of LLM data leakage since SEC-bench CVEs are public and PoVs may already exist online; the authors argue against significant leakage using (a) the fact that DrillAgent substantially outperforms OpenHands under the identical backbone model, and (b) the low PoV-similarity scores in Table 4, though they explicitly call these only 'qualitative indications,' not proof. (3) Benchmark-quality confounds -- e.g., imagemagick.cve-2019-13301 has multiple distinct crash backtraces in its sanitizer report, which can complicate validated-vs-variant triage. (4) Scope limitation -- evaluation focuses almost exclusively on memory-safety vulnerabilities; extension to other vulnerability classes (e.g., command injection, path traversal) is asserted as feasible but not empirically demonstrated in this paper. (5) The single baseline is OpenHands; no comparison is made in this paper against directed-fuzzing-only systems, symbolic-execution hybrids, or other concurrent LLM-agent PoV generators cited in Related Work (PBFuzz, FaultLine, the unnamed coverage-feedback agent [35]) -- those are discussed qualitatively, not benchmarked head-to-head. (6) Ablation set (SEC-bench-Ablation-30) is drawn only from tasks DrillAgent itself solves and is biased toward uniquely-solved cases, which the authors state is intentional for diagnostic clarity but which narrows generalizability of the ablation conclusions.

datasets_used: SEC-bench-Full (190 real-world C/C++ CVE tasks across 29 open-source projects, filtered from SEC-bench's original 200-task suite)
SEC-bench-60 (60-task uniformly random subset of SEC-bench-Full, fixed seed 42)
SEC-bench-Ablation-30 (30-task subset of tasks solved by DrillAgent, prioritized toward uniquely-solved tasks, used for RQ3-RQ5 ablations)
quantitative_results: SEC-bench-Full, $1.5/task budget: DrillAgent = 55 Validated PoVs, 12 Variant PoVs, 67 total crashes, 28.9% resolved rate, 35.3% crash rate

Prior SEC-bench leaderboard best (pre-DrillAgent): 36 solved tasks, 18% resolved rate -> DrillAgent is a 52.8% relative increase (55 vs 36); 29 of DrillAgent's 55 solved tasks were previously unsolved by any method

SEC-bench-60, $1.5/task budget: DrillAgent = 15 Validated PoVs / 2 Variant / 17 total (25.0% resolved, 28.3% crash rate); OpenHands = 6 Validated / 0 Variant / 6 total (10.0% resolved, 10.0% crash rate)
SEC-bench-60, unlimited budget: OpenHands = 18 Validated PoVs (30.0% resolved rate)
Cost per validated PoV (SEC-bench-60): DrillAgent $7.72; OpenHands $15.30 (budgeted) / $20.13 (unlimited)
Execution time per task: DrillAgent ~11.5-11.8 min; OpenHands 3.2 min (budgeted) / 7.7 min (unlimited)

Phase breakdown (Table 3, SEC-bench-Full): Vulnerability Analysis 2.5 min / 228,145 input tok / 8,147 output tok; Path Exploration 3.7 min / 195,908 in / 12,748 out; Crash Triggering 3.3 min / 171,438 in / 11,584 out; Code Instrumentation 1.9 min (tokens omitted)

Average tool usage over 190 tasks: 60 LLM interactions and 100.4 tool invocations per task; read_file 43.8, execute_bash 32, coverage_query 7.8, write_file 5.8, finish 5.4, ast_grep_search_function 5.6 (mean calls/task)

Vulnerability-type distribution of 67 solved tasks (Figure 5): heap-buffer-overflow 26, memory-leak 11, null-pointer-deref 10, heap-use-after-free 7, stack-overflow 6, out-of-bound access 5, uninit-value 1, other 1; spans 13 of 29 SEC-bench projects

Ablation study (SEC-bench-Ablation-30, base DrillAgent-Full = 30 Validated / 0 Variant PoVs): w/o RCA = 15 Validated / 3 Variant; w/o ExecFB (execution/coverage feedback) = 17 Validated / 2 Variant; w/o CrashGuide (CTAgent) = 20 Validated / 2 Variant
PoV similarity to ground truth (Table 4, 24 solved CVEs): GramSim avg 0.0413 (max 0.1886), ChunkSim avg 0.0022 (max 0.0479), combined Score avg 0.0296 (max 0.1320, min 0.0000)
baselines_compared: OpenHands (general-purpose LLM-based agent framework, same backbone model Claude Sonnet 4.5 as DrillAgent for fair comparison), evaluated under both $1.5/task budget and unlimited budget
Prior best reported result on the public SEC-bench leaderboard (36/200 solved, 18% resolved rate) used as an external reference point, not re-run by the authors
